preamble: The shift from the voiceless velar stop [k] to its voiced counterpart [g] is a standard form of voicing lenition. It typically occurs in intervocalic positions where the voiceless stop gains voicing from the surrounding voiced vowels, reducing the effort needed to toggle vocal fold vibration.

examples.note: Latin intervocalic [k] voices to [g] in Spanish.
French historical voicing [k] > [g], then further lenition and elision.
Intervocalic voicing: word-final /k/ voices when a vowel-initial suffix is added.
Word-medial /k/ is lost in the weak grade, shown in alternations like yökkö (darkness) → yön.
Consonant gradation: /k/ voices to [g] (or deletes) in weak grade when preceded by vowel in closed syllable.
Intervocalic voicing: /k/ is realized as [ɡ] between voiced sounds.

Morphologically conditioned intervocalic voicing: stem-final /k/ is voiced to [g] when it appears in intervocalic position (i.e., before a vowel-initial suffix). Voicing occurs only in morpheme-final position, not in suffix-initial position, making this a morphophonological rather than purely phonological process. The Jikany dialect retains final [k] more robustly than other Nuer dialects.

In the Sogeram language family (Trans-New Guinea, Madang Province, Papua New Guinea), Proto-Sogeram *k undergoes lenition word-initially, typically voicing to [g] and in some branches deleting entirely. Don Daniels (2015) identifies this k-lenition as one of the innovations marking the subgrouping of the Sogeram languages. West Sogeram languages extend the process to loss of all word-initial consonants from polysyllabic words.

sources: Penny: A History of the Spanish Language
